headline: 40 Runs, A 9-0 Annihilation, and the Fantasy Baseball Chaos We Came Here For
subheadline: Week 2 of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball delivered a scorched-earth offensive explosion, a stunning sweep, and at least two managers who should probably be stress-eating right now.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### BaseballPro77 def. Benchwarmers, 5-4

Look, not every thriller is a seven-game NBA Finals series. Sometimes a 5-4 fantasy baseball win feels like watching a team grind out a 2-1 soccer match on a muddy pitch in November — technically a victory, spiritually exhausting for everyone involved. **BaseballPro77** survived this one on the strength of pitching and stolen bases, which is the fantasy equivalent of winning a street fight with a combination of good footwork and patience. Their offensive line — 2 HR, a .277 OBP — reads like a middle school report card that somehow passed because the kid aced the science project. Meanwhile, **Benchwarmers** had Corbin Carroll going full superhero mode with 2 HR, 8 RBI, and a .500 OBP, essentially doing the "I am the Senate" speech while the rest of the roster silently let him down. Carroll was a one-man army against a battalion, and he still couldn't get it done. The stakes here are real: both teams are dangerously close to the bottom of the standings, and another week of this kind of inconsistency is a fast track to irrelevance. This was the fantasy equivalent of two teams fighting over the last seat on a lifeboat.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

The **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** didn't just win this week. They sent a message, signed it in blood, and mailed it priority overnight to every other manager in the league. A 9-0 sweep of the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** is the most lopsided result of the season — the kind of scoreline that makes you check if the data is corrupted. It wasn't. Ben Rice put up a monster week with 3 HR, 9 RBI, and a .548 OBP (yes, .548 — that's not a typo, that's a man who has apparently decided hitting is easy), and Mason Miller's relief work was so filthy it should come with a parental advisory label, posting a 0.30 WHIP that would make Greg Maddux blush. The team WHIP of 0.84 for the week is the kind of number that pitching-obsessed managers will screenshot and send to group chats. And they should.

What makes this performance so significant is the context. The **Reapers** have been fighting for legitimacy since the season started — quietly good, never quite loud enough to dominate the conversation. This was their "fine, you want to see what I can do?" moment. This was Daniel Plainview finally getting to drink your milkshake. The rotation was locked in, the offense showed up exactly when it needed to, and the result was the most complete team performance anyone has posted in this league in 2025.

On the other side of that scoreline is a **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** situation that is becoming genuinely alarming. They had three quality starts this week. THREE. Their ERA was a respectable 3.34. And they still got zeroed out in nine categories. Zero. Not one. The offense — 3 HR, 13 RBI, a .270 OBP, zero stolen bases — looked like a team that forgot bats were a required piece of equipment. The pitching-first strategy is only interesting when the pitching staff can carry the weight, but when your rotation is doing its job and you're still getting swept 9-0, the offense isn't just underperforming — it's actively sabotaging everything else. The **Wonderdogs** need to look in the mirror and have some hard conversations. Fast.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Hiroshima Radiation, 8-1

**Sugar Land Skeeters** dropped 40 runs on the **Hiroshima Radiation** this week, which is the statistical equivalent of showing up to a knife fight with a flamethrower. Drake Baldwin and Matt Olson both erupted offensively — this is a lineup that right now doesn't have a soft underbelly. Nine home runs, 30 RBI, a .346 OBP, 67 strikeouts from the pitching staff, and a WHIP of 1.07. That's not a great week; that's a *statement*. The **Radiation** isn't without talent — Carlos Correa and Schwarber are legitimate contributors — but giving up 40 runs while only taking 1 category win is a brutal gut punch. Their pitching (3.29 ERA, 1.17 WHIP) wasn't even terrible; the **Skeeters** offense just doesn't care about your feelings. Three straight top-tier weeks and the **Skeeters** have cemented themselves as the team everyone else is chasing.

### Wisconsin Rapids Reapers def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 9-0

Already covered above in the Defining Moment, but to add the raw context: the **Reapers** posted 75 strikeouts over 61.2 innings with a WHIP of 0.84 — numbers that feel like they belong in a video game set to rookie difficulty. The **Wonderdogs** countered with... 48 strikeouts, 3 HR, 13 RBI, and zero stolen bases. For a team that entered the season with genuine playoff ambitions built around pitching depth, this is a crisis. Next week's schedule cannot come fast enough for **Wonder Wharf**, but the waiver wire additions (more on those below) suggest at least their manager knows something is wrong.

### General Hospital def. Athletic Park Dragons, 6-4

This matchup was a clinic in how to win ugly and still be right. **General Hospital** posted a 2.10 ERA, a 1.02 WHIP, and a .363 OBP — this is a team doing everything correctly and somehow getting docked for it by the schedule gods (more on the luck delta below). The **Athletic Park Dragons** had Judge, Wood, and Torres all contributing, and they still fell short because their pitching — 46 IP, 4.30 ERA, 1.46 WHIP — simply couldn't hold pace. Forty-six innings pitched in a fantasy week is a problem. That's a team that either ran out of starters or is managing innings carefully, neither of which is sustainable in a categories league. **General Hospital** is quietly becoming a top-three team; **Athletic Park** needs to figure out their rotation depth before it costs them a playoff spot.

### North Correa def. Albuquerque Bombers, 7-3

Oneil Cruz put on a show for **North Correa** with 4 HR and 10 RBI, and Ohtani continued doing Ohtani things with a .441 OBP. That combination of power and on-base production is genuinely unfair roster construction. The pitching backed it up with a 2.85 ERA and four quality starts. The **Albuquerque Bombers**, meanwhile, got an encouraging performance from Kyle Harrison (26 K, 1.65 ERA) but still dropped the matchup 7-3 — which tells you how much of the week went sideways everywhere else for them. Their ERA was 4.21 with a 1.36 WHIP outside of Harrison's contribution, and the offensive counting stats (5 HR, 31 RBI) weren't enough to make up the difference. The **Bombers** are a team in active roster construction mode, which is fine, but they need it to work faster than it currently is.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Canberra Wombats, 6-4

The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** quietly put together one of the better weeks in the league — 77 strikeouts tying the all-time single-week record, a 2.25 ERA, seven quality starts, and Yordan Alvarez going nuclear with a .600 OBP, 3 HR, and 8 RBI. (Yes, .600 OBP. Yordan is simply built different.) The **Canberra Wombats** kept it interesting — 31 runs, 9 HR, 32 RBI, and 7 saves/holds — but Seth Lugo's 7.15 ERA is an active disaster. The Wombats have real offensive talent with Altuve, Henderson, and Walker all hitting, but until that pitching stabilizes, they'll keep finding ways to take losses they shouldn't. The **Twins** are rising and deserve more attention than they're getting.

### Riverside Roughnecks def. Buttville Skidmarks, 7-3

**Riverside Roughnecks** are becoming the most quietly terrifying team in this league. Thirty-eight runs, 10 HR, a .365 OBP, and a 2.42 ERA in the same week? That's the full package. The **Buttville Skidmarks** actually pitched well — 3.19 ERA, 1.16 WHIP, four quality starts — and still got beaten handily. That's because the **Skidmarks'** offense (26 runs, .293 OBP) continues to operate at about 60% capacity relative to their potential. Bryan Reynolds is clearly not getting enough help, and a team that pitches this well shouldn't be losing 7-3. Something needs to change on that side of the ball, or the good pitching is just going to keep being wasted.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers.** Their 5-4 win this week had a luck delta of +0.58 — the largest positive deviation in the league. Dig into the underlying numbers and you find bottom-half marks in HR, strikeouts, and innings pitched. Corbin Carroll was so good that he masked a roster that needed masking, and **Benchwarmers** walked away with a win that their category profile, across most possible opponents, probably doesn't deserve. Enjoy it. Spend it wisely. The house always wins eventually.

**Unlucky Team: General Hospital.** A 2.10 ERA. A 1.02 WHIP. A .363 OBP. And a luck delta of -0.52 that reveals they're one of the two most statistically underrewarded teams in the league right now. They won 6-4, sure, but the underlying production is top-three caliber getting dressed up in top-five results. **General Hospital** is the fantasy equivalent of a band that's selling out small venues when they should be headlining festivals. The schedule will correct itself eventually. Probably.

---

## Records and Milestones

The history books got updated twice this week, and weirdly, both records were tied by the teams that set them in the first place, which is either impressive roster consistency or evidence that certain managers found a formula and refuse to mess with it.

**General Hospital** posted a 2.10 ERA this week, tying the lowest ERA ever recorded by a winning team in league history — a record they themselves set in Week 2. Same week, same number, different opponent, same dominance. And **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** matched the all-time single-week strikeout record with 77 K, equaling the mark they set — also in Week 2. Two records, two teams, both holding their own crowns. It's either poetic or the universe is just stuck in a loop. (If you've seen *Dark*, you understand.)

---

## Manager Spotlight: Sugar Land Skeeters

If this league had a preseason favorite, it might not have been the **Sugar Land Skeeters**. But three weeks in, the conversation about who the frontrunner is starts and ends here. Forty runs scored this week — the most by any team in a single week this season. Drake Baldwin and Matt Olson providing the offensive fireworks. A WHIP of 1.07 from a pitching staff that seems to understand its assignment. Seven saves/holds to anchor the bullpen categories.

What makes the **Skeeters** dangerous isn't just that they're winning — it's that they're winning across categories. They're not a one-trick pony relying on one elite skill to carry everything. They hit for power, they get on base, they rack up strikeouts, they close games. That kind of balance is the hardest thing to build in a categories league and the easiest thing to take apart if you're not careful — but right now there's no sign of carelessness here.

The playoff math is obviously early, but three straight weeks of top-tier performance puts this manager in the driver's seat. The **Skeeters** are the team everyone else needs to solve. Nobody has a good answer yet.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Oneil Cruz, North Correa — BUY.** Put up 4 HR and 10 RBI this week while also contributing in the stolen base and OBP categories. Cruz has always had elite tools; if this is the version that finally puts it all together consistently, his current value in trade conversations might still be underestimating him. Buy now before the price goes up.

**Seth Lugo, Canberra Wombats — SELL.** A 7.15 ERA in a week is the kind of performance that gets a starter demoted in real life. The Wombats are holding onto him because of his name value and track record, but if you can flip him for a useful piece while he still carries some name recognition, this is the moment. Sell before the market corrects.

**Corbin Carroll, Benchwarmers — HOLD.** Yes, he was sensational — 2 HR, 8 RBI, .500 OBP, carrying his entire team on his back. But the **Benchwarmers** roster context means he'll keep getting asked to do too much, which inflates both his perceived value and the actual pressure on his performance. Hold him if you have him, but don't overpay to acquire him based on one heroic week.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | League-best 40 R, 8-1 blowout, Drake Baldwin and Matt Olson both erupted — three straight top-tier weeks cements top spot [SAME]
2. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | 9-0 sweep is the statement of the season; Ben Rice, Mason Miller, and a 0.84 WHIP rotation demand respect [UP]
3. **General Hospital** | 2.10 ERA, .363 OBP, and a 6-4 win despite -0.52 luck delta — this team is quietly undervalued and primed to surge [SAME]
4. **BaseballPro77** | Ugly offensive week (2 HR, .277 OBP) but pitching bailed them out; a 5-4 win is a 5-4 win and they remain elite on the mound [DOWN]
5. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Yordan Alvarez went nuclear (.600 OBP, 3 HR, 8 RBI), league-record-tying 77 K, and a 2.25 ERA — Merrill Kelly add could be pivotal [UP]
6. **North Correa** | Oneil Cruz (4 HR, 10 RBI) and Ohtani (.441 OBP) powered a 7-3 win; pitching ERA of 2.85 rounds out a balanced roster [SAME]
7. **Riverside Roughnecks** | 38 runs, 10 HR, and a 7-3 victory — this team has quietly become one of the most explosive offenses in the league [UP]
8. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Lost 6-4 despite Judge, Wood, and Torres all contributing — thin pitching (46 IP) remains the Achilles heel [DOWN]
9. **Hiroshima Radiation** | 8-1 dismantling is a brutal wake-up call; Carlos Correa and Schwarber are fine but the team gave up 40 runs and only 1 category [DOWN]
10. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Lost 7-3 but Kyle Harrison (26 K, 1.65 ERA) was a revelation; aggressive waiver activity shows a manager fighting back [SAME]
11. **Canberra Wombats** | Altuve (.577 OBP), Henderson (3 HR), and Walker (3 HR) all hit — but pitching (Seth Lugo 7.15 ERA) remains a disaster [UP]
12. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Lost 7-3 despite solid pitching (3.19 ERA, 4 QS); offense is too passive and Reynolds' production is being wasted [DOWN]
13. **Benchwarmers** | Carroll was a one-man army but the roster around him is still thin; barely escaped with a 5-4 win against a struggling opponent [SAME]
14. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | A 9-0 shutout loss with zero category wins is as bad as it gets; the offense (3 HR, 13 RBI) is an emergency [DOWN]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[B] Albuquerque Bombers** — Added Keaton Winn, Braxton Garrett, Josh Jung, Noelvi Marte — After Kyle Harrison's breakout (26 K, 1.65 ERA), bolstering the rotation and corner infield depth shows smart roster building. Garrett and Winn give real upside if they land regular starts, and the aggressive four-player haul signals a manager who saw one week of hope and decided to go all in. Smart urgency, even if some of these names are still unproven at this level.

**[B+] General Hospital** — Added Kumar Rocker; dropped Jack Leiter, Janson Junk, Cristian Javier — Clearing out the rotation dead weight and inserting Rocker is a high-upside gamble that fits exactly what a contending team should be doing. If Rocker develops into a reliable starter, this already elite pitching staff becomes nearly unbeatable. The drops are the right call — Leiter and Junk were roster spots masquerading as contributors. Best move of the week.

**[B] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Masyn Winn, Dustin May, Ildemaro Vargas, Scott Barlow — A necessary roster overhaul after a 9-0 shutout, and at minimum it shows the manager isn't asleep at the wheel. May adds rotation depth and Masyn Winn directly addresses the speed and OBP gaps that got exposed this week. The concern is timing — these changes needed to happen a week ago, and the **Wonderdogs** can't afford another week like this while the new pieces get comfortable.

**[C+] North Correa** — Added Austin Wells, Brayan Rocchio; dropped J.T. Realmuto, Brooks Lee — Upgrading at catcher is sensible in theory, but dropping Realmuto is the kind of move that looks fine until it suddenly doesn't. Wells needs to earn his keep quickly on a roster already relying heavily on Cruz and Ohtani to carry the offensive load. A calculated risk on a team that can afford one — for now.

**[C] Riverside Roughnecks** — Added Victor Vodnik; dropped Mark Vientos — A minor bullpen add that makes sense given their thin saves/holds production, but losing Vientos' power upside at corner infield is a real trade-off for a team whose identity is built entirely on offensive explosion. If Vodnik doesn't immediately contribute in the saves/holds categories, this is going to look like a mistake pretty quickly. The **Roughnecks** are winning on offense — don't sacrifice that for a bullpen piece who may never get a save opportunity.
